key_insight: Tidally driven rock grinding in Enceladus’s core could generate enough molecular hydrogen to explain the plume observations, especially if freshly fractured rock is repeatedly exposed to water.
engineering_relevance: The study links fracture/damage mechanics and energy dissipation to geochemical fuel production, showing how mechanical failure can control habitability-relevant chemistry in icy moon interiors.
method: Use laboratory measurements of H2 release from freshly fractured rock-water reactions.
Scale H2 production by the fraction of tidal energy dissipated through rock grinding.
Compare predicted H2 rates against radiolysis and serpentinization scenarios.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports model-based estimates grounded in laboratory fracture experiments, concluding that tidal grinding can plausibly match the observed trace H2 in Enceladus’s plume.
watch_item: The mechanism depends on efficient healing of silicate surfaces; without it, H2 generation may be episodic rather than sustained over geologic timescales.
paper_title: Tidal Rock Grinding as a Source of H2 on Enceladus
